# CC4101 Tarea 3

## Typechecking y Pattern Matching

Para la resolución de la tarea recuerde que

                   Toda función debe estar acompañada de su firma, una breve descripción coloquial (en T3.rkt) y un conjunto significativo de tests (en test.rkt).
                   Todo datatype definido por el usuario (via deftype) debe estar acompañado de
                   una breve descripción coloquial y de la gramática BNF que lo genera.

              Si la función o el datatype no cumple con estas reglas, será ignorado.

         Ejercicio 1                                                                             30 Pt

                (a) [2 Pt] En el archivo T3.rkt se encuentra la definición del tipo de datos Type, que
                    representa los tipos válidos para un lenguaje con valores numéricos, booleanos
                    y funciones de 1°clase (por simplicidad de un solo argumento). Siguiendo los
                    ejemplos, defina la función recursiva
                    ;; parse-type ::   <s-type> -> Type

                    >>> (parse-type 'Number)
                    (numT)
                    >>> (parse-type 'Boolean)
                    (boolT)
                    >>> (parse-type '(-> Number Boolean))
                    (arrowT (numT) (boolT))
                    >>> (parse-type 'String)
                    parse-type: invalid type String

                (b) [4 Pt] En el mismo archivo, se encuentra la definición base de un lenguaje con
                    valores numéricos y booleanos. Extienda la gramática BNF del lenguaje y el
                    tipo de datos Expr para que este soporte la definición y aplicación de funciones
                    de 1°clase. Considere que como se quiere implementar typechecking, al definir
                    funciones se debe indicar explícitamente el tipo del argumento. Puede guiarse
                    con los siguientes ejemplos para la sintaxis concreta y abstracta (aún no necesita
                    definir la función parse):
                    >>> (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
                    (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))
                    >>> (parse '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10))
                    (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10))

(c) [7 Pt] Escriba la función
    ;; parse ::   <s-expr> -> Expr
    que transforma un programa en sintaxis concreta a uno en sintaxis abstracta.

(d) [7 Pt] Implemente la función
    ;; check-type ::   Expr TypeEnv -> Type
    que realiza la comprobación de tipos (typechecking) sobre un programa en sintaxis
    abstracta. Para ello, considere las siguientes reglas:

        Las constantes numéricas son de tipo numérico (numT).

        Las constantes booleanas ( tt y ff) son de tipo boolean (boolT).

        En el caso de la suma y la multiplicación, estas tienen tipo numérico
        (numT), si y solo si, ambos operandos tienen tipo numérico (numT). Por
        otro lado, la operación “menor que” es de tipo boolean (boolT) si ambos
        operandos son de tipo numérico.

        Un identificador obtiene su tipo a partir de la extracción de un ambiente de
        tipos (TypeEnv).

        En el caso de los condicionales (if ), la condición debe tener tipo boolT.
        Por otro lado, ambas ramas de ejecución deben tener el mismo tipo entre sí,
        siendo este el tipo que recibe el condicional completo.

        Las funciones tienen su propio tipo (arrowT) que indica el tipo del dominio
        y el tipo del codominio. En el caso del primero, las funciones son declaradas
        con el tipo del argumento, por lo que se conoce de antemano. Por otro lado,
        para el codominio se debe inferir el tipo extendiendo el ambiente de tipos
        con el argumento de la función.

        La aplicación de una función tiene el tipo del codominio si el argumento que
        se está entregando es del tipo indicado en la declaración de la función.

    Cuando las reglas anteriores no se cumplen, la función debe arrojar errores, para
    ello, siga estos ejemplos:
    >>> (check-type (add (tt) (num 50)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Addition expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type (mul (num 10) (ff)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Multiplication expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type (lt (num 25) (ff)) empty-tenv)
    check-type: Less-than expects 2 numbers
    >>> (check-type ( ite (num 100) (tt) (ff )) empty-tenv)
    check-type: If condition must be a boolean
    >>> (check-type ( ite (tt) (num 500) (ff)) empty-tenv)

        check-type: If branches type mismatch
        >>> (check-type (app (num 100) (num 300)) empty-tenv)
        check-type: Function application to a non-function
        >>> (check-type (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id x) (num 2))) (ff)) empty-tenv)
        check-type: Function argument type mismatch

    (e) [7 Pt] Escriba la función
        ;; interp ::   Expr Env -> Value
        que evalúa un Expr hacia un valor final del lenguaje. Asuma que el programa
        (Expr) está bien tipado.

    (f) [3 Pt] Implemente la función
        ;; run ::   <s-expr> -> Value
        que reduce programas en sintaxis concreta a valores del lenguaje, si y solo si, el
        programa está bien tipado.
        Hint: Le puede ser de utilidad la función begin de Racket.
Ejercicio 2                                                                          30 Pt

    (a) [6 Pt] En el mismo archivo (T3.rkt) se define el tipo de datos Prog como un
        lenguaje básico con números, adición, un constructor de pares (conz) y el constructor nil . Implemente la función
        ;; parser ::   <s-prog> -> Prog
        e incluya azúcar sintáctico para definir listas a partir de los constructores conz
        y nil . Puede seguir los ejemplos:
        >>> (parser '( nil ))
        ( nil )
        >>> (parser '(cons 1 2))
        (conz (numbr 1) (numbr 2))
        >>> (parser '(list 1 2 3))
        (conz (numbr 1) (conz (numbr 2) (conz (numbr 3) (nil))))

    (b) [4 Pt] Como se va a implementar pattern matching, se define el tipo de datos
        Pattern. Escriba la función
        ;; parse-pattern ::   <s-prog> -> Pattern
        siguiendo los ejemplos:
        >>> (parse-pattern '3)
        (numP 3)
        >>> (parse-pattern 'x)
        (varP 'x)
        >>> (parse-pattern '( nil ))
        (nilP)
        >>> (parse-pattern '(cons 1 2))

    (conzP (numP 1) (numP 2))
    >>> (parse-pattern '(list 1 2 3))
    (conzP (numP 1) (conzP (numP 2) (conzP (numP 3) (nilP))))

(c) [6 Pt] El desafío de implementar un lenguaje con pattern matching es determinar
    si un valor calza con un patrón dado, encontrando además la asociación entre
    variable y valor. Por ejemplo, el valor (cons (1 (cons 2 3))) calza con el patrón
    (cons x y), en donde x se asocia con 1 e y con (cons 2 3). Por otro lado, el valor
    (cons 2 3) no calza con el patrón (cons x ( nil )) , pues 2 no corresponde con el
    patrón ( nil ).
    En T3.rkt se define el tipo de datos Result que permite retornar un resultado
    exitoso o un mensaje de error sin la necesidad de arrojar una excepción. De esta
    manera, se puede hacer un manejo explícito de los errores. Por ejemplo, se puede
    definir una “división segura”:
    ;; safe-div :: Number Number -> (Result String Number)
    ( define ( safe-div x y)
       (if (zero? y) ( failure "DivisionByZero") (success (/ x y))))
    Bajo esta idea, implemente la función
    ;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol *
         PValue)))
    que a partir de un patrón y un valor, retorna una lista de substituciones inducidas
    por el pattern matching o un mensaje de error si el match no es exitoso. Para
    ello, asuma que:

        nil es un valor (nilV)

        Un par construido con conz es un valor (conzV) solo si sus componentes son
        valores.

        En un patrón nunca se repite el nombre de una variable.

    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 3))
    (success '())
    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 4))
    ( failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern")
    >>> (generate-substs (varP 'x) (numerical 3))
    (success (list (cons 'x (numV 3))))
    >>> (generate-substs (numP 3) (nilV))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected a number")
    >>> (generate-substs (nilP) (numerical 45))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected nil")
    >>> (generate-substs (conzP (varP 'x) (nilP)) (numerical 22))
    ( failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")

(d) [6 Pt] Escriba la función

    ;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue
   recordando las características de los valores (PValue) mencionados en la sección
   anterior.

(e) [6 Pt] Extienda la gramática BNF del lenguaje, el tipo Prog, la función parser y
    la función reduce para que soporten la expresión match. Siga los ejemplos:
   >>> (parser '(match 2 [1 1] [x 3]))
   (pmatch (numbr 2) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1)) (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
   >>> (parser '(match (list 1 2 3)))
   SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case
   >>> (reduce (pmatch (numbr 2) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1)) (cons (varP 'x) (
       numbr 3)))) '())
   (numerical 3)
   >>> (reduce (pmatch (nil) (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 10)) (cons (numP 2) (
       numbr 20)))) '())
   MatchError: expression does not match any pattern

(f) [2 Pt] ¿Por qué es útil que generate-substs no arroje una excepción sino que
    retorne un mensaje de error?

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture of the task. Let me analyze what's needed:

## Exercise 1 (30 pts)

### Part 1a (2 pts): `parse-type`
Parse s-expressions into `Type`:
- `'Number` → `(numT)`
- `'Boolean` → `(boolT)`
- `'(-> Number Boolean)` → `(arrowT (numT) (boolT))`
- Invalid type → error

### Part 1b (4 pts): Extend grammar for functions
Add function definition (`fun`) and application (`app`) to the grammar and `Expr` type.

The concrete syntax:
- `(fun x Number (+ x 1))` → `(fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))`
- `((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10)` → `(app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10))`

So we need to add:
- `<s-expr> ::= (list fun <symbol> <s-type> <s-expr>)`
- `<s-expr> ::= (list <s-expr> <s-expr>)`  (application)

And to `Expr`:
- `(fun id type body)`
- `(app func arg)`

### Part 1c (7 pts): `parse`
Transform concrete syntax (s-expr) to abstract syntax (Expr).

### Part 1d (7 pts): `check-type`
Type checking with error messages.

### Part 1e (7 pts): `interp`
Evaluation.

### Part 1f (3 pts): `run`
Parse, type-check, and evaluate.

## Exercise 2 (30 pts)

### Part 2a (6 pts): `parser` for `Prog` with list sugar
Parse s-expr into `Prog`. Include sugar for `(list ...)` → nested `conz`.

### Part 2b (4 pts): `parse-pattern`
Parse patterns.

### Part 2c (6 pts): `generate-substs`
Pattern matching with `Result` type.

### Part 2d (6 pts): `reduce`
Evaluate `Prog` expressions.

### Part 2e (6 pts): Extend with `match` expression
Add `pmatch` to `Prog`, extend parser and reduce.

### Part 2f (2 pts): Why use Result instead of exceptions?
Answer in Spanish.

Let me now implement all of this in T3.rkt.

First, let me check what `#lang play` is - it's likely a teaching language used at universities. It should support `deftype` and `match` as shown in the file.

Let me start implementing.

For Part 1b, I need to update:
- The BNF grammar comments
- The `Expr` deftype to include `fun` and `app`

For parsing, I need to handle:
- Numbers → `(num <number>)`
- `true` → `(tt)`
- `false` → `(ff)`
- Symbols → `(id <symbol>)`
- `(+ e1 e2)` → `(add e1 e2)`
- `(* e1 e2)` → `(mul e1 e2)`
- `(< e1 e2)` → `(lt e1 e2)`
- `(if e1 e2 e3)` → `(ite e1 e2 e3)`
- `(fun x type body)` → `(fun 'x (parse-type type) body)`
- `(e1 e2)` → `(app e1 e2)` — this is tricky because `(fun ...)` and `(if ...)` etc. also use parentheses. I need to check if it's an application.
  
Wait, let me look at the examples more carefully:

```
>>> (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
(fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))
>>> (parse '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10))
(app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10))
```

So `(fun x Number body)` is a special form, and `(e1 e2)` where e1 is not a special form is an application. This is similar to Scheme's syntax.

So the parsing logic:
1. If it's a number → `(num n)`
2. If it's `true` → `(tt)`
3. If it's `false` → `(ff)`
4. If it's a symbol (that's not true/false) → `(id sym)`
5. If it's a list:
   - First element is `+` → `(add <parse left> <parse right>)`
   - First element is `*` → `(mul <parse left> <parse right>)`
   - First element is `<` → `(lt <parse left> <parse right>)`
   - First element is `if` → `(ite <parse cond> <parse true> <parse false>)`
   - First element is `fun` → `(fun '<arg-name> (parse-type <arg-type>) <parse body>)`
   - Otherwise → `(app <parse e1> <parse e2>)` where e1 and e2 are the two elements.

Wait, but what about `(fun x Number (+ x 1))`? The `s-expr` is `(fun x Number (+ x 1))`. The fun form has 4 elements: `fun`, `x`, `Number`, `(+ x 1)`.

But in the application case `((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10)`, the list has 2 elements: `(fun x Number (* x 2))` and `10`. So I need to check if it's a 2-element list where the first element is not a special form.

Actually, looking more carefully at the BNF:
```
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | true
           | false
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list * <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list < <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | (list if <s-expr> <s-expr> <s-expr>)
           | ...
```

The "..." parts are what I need to add. For fun and app:
```
           | (list fun <symbol> <s-type> <s-expr>)
           | (list <s-expr> <s-expr>)
```

So when parsing a list:
- If the first element is `+`, `*`, `<`, `if`, `fun` → special form
- Otherwise, it should be a 2-element list representing application

Let me now implement all of this.

For `parse-type`:
```racket
(define (parse-type stype)
  (match stype
    ['Number (numT)]
    ['Boolean (boolT)]
    [(list '-> t1 t2) (arrowT (parse-type t1) (parse-type t2))]
    [_ (error 'parse-type "invalid type ~a" stype)]))
```

For `parse`:
```racket
(define (parse sexpr)
  (match sexpr
    [(? number? n) (num n)]
    [(? boolean? b) (if b (tt) (ff))]
    [(? symbol? s) (id s)]
    [(list '+ l r) (add (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '* l r) (mul (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '< l r) (lt (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list 'if c t e) (ite (parse c) (parse t) (parse e))]
    [(list 'fun x t body) (fun x (parse-type t) (parse body))]
    [(list e1 e2) (app (parse e1) (parse e2))]
    [_ (error 'parse "invalid syntax: ~a" sexpr)]))
```

Wait, but `true` and `false` in the concrete syntax... The examples don't show them. Let me check the BNF again:

```
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | true
           | false
           | <symbol>
```

So `true` and `false` are literal symbols that need to be treated specially. But in Racket/Scheme, `true` and `false` are symbols. However, `#t` and `#f` are booleans. The spec says `true` and `false`, which are symbols.

Actually, looking at the examples in part D:
```
>>> (check-type (add (tt) (num 50)) empty-tenv)
```

These are abstract syntax expressions, not concrete. So we don't have concrete examples with `true` and `false`.

But for parsing, `true` should become `(tt)` and `false` should become `(ff)`. In the parse function, I'll treat them specially.

Wait, but if I check `(? boolean? b)`, that works for `#t` and `#f`, but the concrete syntax uses `true` and `false` which are symbols. Let me check how the BNF is written...

In the BNF:
```
<s-expr> ::= <number>
           | true
           | false
           | <symbol>
```

So `true` and `false` are literal symbols in the concrete syntax. In Racket, when we write `'true` or `'false`, they're symbols. So I need to match symbols named 'true' and 'false'.

Let me adjust:

```racket
(define (parse sexpr)
  (match sexpr
    [(? number? n) (num n)]
    ['true (tt)]
    ['false (ff)]
    [(? symbol? s) (id s)]
    [(list '+ l r) (add (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '* l r) (mul (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list '< l r) (lt (parse l) (parse r))]
    [(list 'if c t e) (ite (parse c) (parse t) (parse e))]
    [(list 'fun x t body) (fun x (parse-type t) (parse body))]
    [(list e1 e2) (app (parse e1) (parse e2))]
    [_ (error 'parse "invalid syntax: ~a" sexpr)]))
```

But there's a problem: in the examples, `(fun 'x (numT) ...)` shows that the fun's first field is a symbol (quoted), so I need to store it as a symbol in the abstract syntax. And `(id 'x)` also stores a symbol.

Wait, looking at the example:
```
>>> (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
(fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1)))
```

So `(fun 'x ...)` - the x is quoted, meaning the fun's first argument is a symbol. Similarly, `(id 'x)` means id's field is a symbol.

In the deftype:
```racket
(deftype Expr
  (num n)
  (tt)
  (ff)
  (id x)
  (add left right)
  (mul left right)
  (lt left right)
  (ite cond true false)
  '...)
```

I need to add `(fun id type body)` and `(app func arg)`. The `fun`'s `id` would be a symbol, `type` would be a `Type`, and `body` an `Expr`.

Now for `check-type`:

I need to implement type checking with the given rules.

For `interp`:

I need to evaluate expressions. With closures for function values.

For `run`:
```racket
(define (run sexpr)
  (let* ([e (parse sexpr)]
         [t (check-type e empty-tenv)]
         [v (interp e empty-env)])

;; parser :: <s-prog> -> Prog
(define (parser sprog)
  (match sprog
    [(? number? n) (numbr n)]
    [(? symbol? s) (variable s)]
    [(list '+ l r) (plus (parser l) (parser r))]
    [(list 'nil) (nil)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conz (parser a) (parser b))]
    [(list 'list . args) (parse-list args)]
    [(list 'match expr . cases)
     (when (null? cases) (error "SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case"))
     (pmatch (parser expr) (map parse-case cases))]
    [_ (error 'parser "invalid syntax: ~a" sprog)]))

(define (parse-list args)
  (if (null? args)
      (nil)
      (conz (parser (car args)) (parse-list (cdr args)))))

(define (parse-case case)
  (match case
    [(list pat body) (cons (parse-pattern pat) (parser body))]
    [_ (error "SyntaxError: invalid match case: ~a" case)]))
```

```racket
;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol * PValue)))
(define (generate-substs pat val)
  (match pat
    [(numP n)
     (match val
       [(numerical m) (if (= n m) (success '()) (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern"))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a number")])]
    [(nilP)
     (match val
       [(nilV) (success '())]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected nil")])]
    [(varP x)
     (success (list (cons x val)))]
    [(conzP a b)
     (match val
       [(conzV va vb)
        (let ([ra (generate-substs a va)]
              [rb (generate-substs b vb)])
          (match ra
            [(failure e) (failure e)]
            [(success sa)
             (match rb
               [(failure e) (failure e)]
               [(success sb) (success (append sa sb))])]))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")])]))
```

  (match prog
    [(numbr n) (numerical n)]
    [(nil) (nilV)]
    [(conz a b) (conzV (reduce a subst) (reduce b subst))]
    [(variable x)

    [(plus l r) (num+ (reduce l subst) (reduce r subst))]
    [(pmatch expr cases)

         [(success subs) (reduce body (append subs subst))]
         [(failure _) (match-cases val rest subst)]))]))

;; parser :: <s-prog> -> Prog
(define (parser sprog)
  (match sprog
    [(? number? n) (numbr n)]
    [(? symbol? s) (variable s)]
    [(list '+ l r) (plus (parser l) (parser r))]
    [(list 'nil) (nil)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conz (parser a) (parser b))]
    [(list 'list . args) (parse-list args)]
    [(list 'match expr . cases)
     (if (null? cases)
         (error "SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case")
         (pmatch (parser expr) (map parse-case cases)))]
    [_ (error 'parser "invalid syntax: ~a" sprog)]))

(define (parse-list args)
  (if (null? args)
      (nil)
      (conz (parser (car args)) (parse-list (cdr args)))))

(define (parse-case case)
  (match case
    [(list pat body) (cons (parse-pattern pat) (parser body))]
    [_ (error "SyntaxError: invalid case: ~a" case)]))

;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol * PValue)))
(define (generate-substs pat val)
  (match pat
    [(numP n)
     (match val
       [(numerical m) (if (= n m) (success '()) (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern"))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a number")])]
    [(nilP)
     (match val
       [(nilV) (success '())]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected nil")])]
    [(varP x)
     (success (list (cons x val)))]
    [(conzP a b)
     (match val
       [(conzV va vb)
        (let ([ra (generate-substs a va)]
              [rb (generate-substs b vb)])
          (match ra
            [(failure e) (failure e)]
            [(success sa)
             (match rb
               [(failure e) (failure e)]
               [(success sb) (success (append sa sb))])]))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")])]))

;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue
(define (reduce prog subst)
  (match prog
    [(numbr n) (numerical n)]
    [(nil) (nilV)]
    [(conz a b) (conzV (reduce a subst) (reduce b subst))]
    [(variable x)
     (match (assoc x subst)
       [(cons _ v) v]
       [#f (error 'reduce "unbound variable: ~a" x)])]
    [(plus l r) (num+ (reduce l subst) (reduce r subst))]
    [(pmatch expr cases)
     (let ([val (reduce expr subst)])
       (match-cases val cases subst))]))

(define (match-cases val cases subst)
  (match cases
    ['() (error "MatchError: expression does not match any pattern")]
    [(cons (cons pat body) rest)
     (let ([res (generate-substs pat val)])
       (match res
         [(success subs) (reduce body (append subs subst))]
         [(failure _) (match-cases val rest subst)]))]))

Respuesta: Es útil que generate-substs no arroje una excepción sino que retorne
un mensaje de error (a través del tipo Result) porque permite un manejo explícito
de errores sin interrumpir el flujo de control. En particular, para el pattern
matching en la función reduce, cuando un patrón no calza con un valor, se puede
probar el siguiente caso de manera natural, sin necesidad de usar manejadores de
excepciones (como with-handlers). Esto hace que el código sea más predecible y
más fácil de razonar, ya que los errores se tratan como valores regulares y el
programador decide explícitamente qué hacer cuando ocurre un fallo en el match.

     (let ([lt (check-type l env)]
           [rt (check-type r env)])
       (match lt
         [(numT) (match rt
                   [(numT) (numT)]
                   [_ (error 'check-type "Multiplication expects 2 numbers")])]
         [_ (error 'check-type "Multiplication expects 2 numbers")]))]

           [rt (check-type r env)])

                   [(numT) (boolT)]
                   [_ (error 'check-type "Less-than expects 2 numbers")])]
         [_ (error 'check-type "Less-than expects 2 numbers")]))]
    [(ite c t e)

           [et (check-type e env)])

         [(boolT) (if (equal? tt et) tt (error 'check-type "If branches type mismatch"))]

#| Parte E |#

#| Language Values |#
;; numV y boolV son wrappers de los valores de Racket.
(deftype Value
  (numV n)
  (boolV v)
  (closureV arg body env))

#| Env Utils |#
;; Recuerde que los ambientes guardan valores, no expresiones.
(deftype Env
  (mtEnv)
  (xtEnv id val env))

(define empty-env (mtEnv))

(define extend-env xtEnv)

(define (lookup x env)
  (match env
    [(mtEnv) (error 'lookup "free identifier: ~a" x)]
    [(xtEnv id val rest) (if (symbol=? x id) val (lookup x rest))]))

;; interp :: Expr Env -> Value
(define (interp expr env)
  (match expr
    [(num n) (numV n)]
    [(tt) (boolV #t)]
    [(ff) (boolV #f)]
    [(id x) (lookup x env)]
    [(add l r)
     (let ([lv (interp l env)]
           [rv (interp r env)])
       (match lv
         [(numV n) (match rv
                     [(numV m) (numV (+ n m))])]))]
    [(mul l r)
     (let ([lv (interp l env)]
           [rv (interp r env)])
       (match lv
         [(numV n) (match rv
                     [(numV m) (numV (* n m))])]))]
    [(lt l r)
     (let ([lv (interp l env)]
           [rv (interp r env)])
       (match lv
         [(numV n) (match rv
                     [(numV m) (boolV (< n m))])]))]
    [(ite c t e)
     (let ([cv (interp c env)])
       (match cv
         [(boolV v) (if v (interp t env) (interp e env))]))]
    [(fun id type body)
     (closureV id body env)]
    [(app func arg)
     (let ([fv (interp func env)]
           [av (interp arg env)])
       (match fv
         [(closureV id body fenv) (interp body (xtEnv id av fenv))]))]))

;; run :: <s-expr> -> Value
(define (run sexpr)
  (let* ([expr (parse sexpr)]
         [typ (check-type expr empty-tenv)]
         [val (interp expr empty-env)])
    val))

;;==== P2 ==== ;;
;;------------ ;;

#| Parte A y E |#

#|
<prog> ::= (numbr <number>)
         | (variable <symbol>)
         | (plus <prog> <prog>)
         | (nil)
         | (conz <prog> <prog>)
         | (pmatch <prog> (listof (cons <pattern> <prog>)))
|#

#|
<s-prog> ::= <number>
           | <symbol>
           | (list + <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list nil)
           | (list cons <s-prog> <s-prog>)
           | (list list <s-prog> ...)
           | (list match <s-prog> (list <pattern> <s-prog>) ...)
|#

;; parser :: <s-prog> -> Prog
(define (parser sprog)
  (match sprog
    [(? number? n) (numbr n)]
    [(? symbol? s) (variable s)]
    [(list '+ l r) (plus (parser l) (parser r))]
    [(list 'nil) (nil)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conz (parser a) (parser b))]
    [(list 'list . args) (parse-list args)]
    [(list 'match expr . cases)
     (if (null? cases)
         (error "SyntaxError: match expression must have at least one case")
         (pmatch (parser expr) (map parse-case cases)))]
    [_ (error 'parser "invalid syntax: ~a" sprog)]))

(define (parse-list args)
  (if (null? args)
      (nil)
      (conz (parser (car args)) (parse-list (cdr args)))))

(define (parse-case case)
  (match case
    [(list pat body) (cons (parse-pattern pat) (parser body))]
    [_ (error "SyntaxError: invalid case: ~a" case)]))


#| Parte B |#

#|
<pattern> ::= (numP <number>)
            | (varP <symbol>)
            | (nilP)
            | (conzP <pattern> <pattern>)
|#
(deftype Pattern
  (numP n)
  (varP x)
  (nilP)
  (conzP a b))

;; parse-pattern :: <s-prog> -> Pattern
(define (parse-pattern sp)
  (match sp
    [(? number? n) (numP n)]
    [(? symbol? s) (varP s)]
    [(list 'nil) (nilP)]
    [(list 'cons a b) (conzP (parse-pattern a) (parse-pattern b))]
    [(list 'list . args) (parse-pattern-list args)]
    [_ (error 'parse-pattern "invalid pattern: ~a" sp)]))

(define (parse-pattern-list args)
  (if (null? args)
      (nilP)
      (conzP (parse-pattern (car args)) (parse-pattern-list (cdr args)))))


#| Parte C |#

#|
<pvalue> ::= (numerical <number>)
          | (nilV)
          | (conzV <value> <value>)
|#
(deftype PValue
  (numerical n)
  (nilV)
  (conzV a b))

#|
<result> e v ::= (failure e)
               | (success v)
|#
(deftype Result
  (failure e)
  (success v))

;; generate-substs :: Pattern PValue -> (Result String (Listof (Symbol * PValue)))
(define (generate-substs pat val)
  (match pat
    [(numP n)
     (match val
       [(numerical m) (if (= n m) (success '()) (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern"))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a number")])]
    [(nilP)
     (match val
       [(nilV) (success '())]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected nil")])]
    [(varP x)
     (success (list (cons x val)))]
    [(conzP a b)
     (match val
       [(conzV va vb)
        (let ([ra (generate-substs a va)]
              [rb (generate-substs b vb)])
          (match ra
            [(failure e) (failure e)]
            [(success sa)
             (match rb
               [(failure e) (failure e)]
               [(success sb) (success (append sa sb))])]))]
       [_ (failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")])]))


#| Parte D y E |#

;; num+ PValue PValue -> PValue
(define (num+ v1 v2)
  (match v1
    [(numerical n) (match v2
                [(numerical m) (numerical (+ n m))]
                [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")])]
    [_ (error "TypeError: expected a number")]))

;; reduce :: Prog (Listof (Symbol * PValue)) -> PValue
(define (reduce prog subst)
  (match prog
    [(numbr n) (numerical n)]
    [(nil) (nilV)]
    [(conz a b) (conzV (reduce a subst) (reduce b subst))]
    [(variable x)
     (match (assoc x subst)
       [(cons _ v) v]
       [#f (error 'reduce "unbound variable: ~a" x)])]
    [(plus l r) (num+ (reduce l subst) (reduce r subst))]
    [(pmatch expr cases)
     (let ([val (reduce expr subst)])
       (match-cases val cases subst))]))

(define (match-cases val cases subst)
  (match cases
    ['() (error "MatchError: expression does not match any pattern")]
    [(cons (cons pat body) rest)
     (let ([res (generate-substs pat val)])
       (match res
         [(success subs) (reduce body (append subs subst))]
         [(failure _) (match-cases val rest subst)]))]))


#| Parte F |#

#|
Respuesta: Es útil que generate-substs no arroje una excepción sino que retorne
un mensaje de error (a través del tipo Result) porque permite un manejo explícito
de errores sin interrumpir el flujo de control. En particular, para el pattern
matching en la función reduce, cuando un patrón no calza con un valor, se puede
probar el siguiente caso de manera natural, sin necesidad de usar manejadores de
excepciones (como with-handlers). Esto hace que el código sea más predecible y
más fácil de razonar, ya que los errores se tratan como valores regulares y el
programador decide explícitamente qué hacer cuando ocurre un fallo en el match.
|#

(check-expect (parse 'false) (ff))

(check-expect (parse 'foo) (id 'foo))

(check-expect (parse '(* 3 4)) (mul (num 3) (num 4)))

              (ite (tt) (num 1) (num 2)))

;; fun
(check-expect (parse '(fun x Number (+ x 1)))
              (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1))))
(check-expect (parse '(fun b Boolean (if b 1 0)))
              (fun 'b (boolT) (ite (id 'b) (num 1) (num 0))))

;; app
(check-expect (parse '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10))
              (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (num 10)))

(require "T3.rkt")

;; Test helpers
(define passed 0)

(define (test name expected actual)
  (if (equal? expected actual)
      (begin (set! passed (+ passed 1))
             (displayln (string-append "  PASS: " name)))
      (begin (set! failed (+ failed 1))
             (displayln (string-append "  FAIL: " name
                                       " (expected " (format "~a" expected)
                                       " got " (format "~a" actual) ")")))))

(define (test-error name thunk)
  (with-handlers ([exn:fail? (lambda (e) (set! passed (+ passed 1))
                                (displayln (string-append "  PASS: " name " (error caught)")))])
    (thunk)
    (set! failed (+ failed 1))
    (displayln (string-append "  FAIL: " name " (no error thrown)"))))

  (test-error "parse if arity" (lambda () (parse '(if 1 2))))

  (displayln "=== P1.D check-type ===")
  (test "num type" (numT) (check-type (num 42) empty-tenv))
  (test "true type" (boolT) (check-type (tt) empty-tenv))
  (test "false type" (boolT) (check-type (ff) empty-tenv))
  (test "add type" (numT) (check-type (add (num 1) (num 2)) empty-tenv))
  (test "mul type" (numT) (check-type (mul (num 3) (num 4)) empty-tenv))
  (test "lt type" (boolT) (check-type (lt (num 5) (num 6)) empty-tenv))
  (test "if (num branches)" (numT) (check-type (ite (tt) (num 1) (num 2)) empty-tenv))
  (test "if (bool branches)" (boolT) (check-type (ite (ff) (tt) (ff)) empty-tenv))
  (test "fun type" (arrowT (numT) (numT))
        (check-type (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1))) empty-tenv))
  (test "app type" (numT)
        (check-type (app (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1))) (num 5)) empty-tenv))
  (test "id in env" (numT) (check-type (id 'x) (xTenv 'x (numT) empty-tenv)))

  ;; Errores
  (test-error "add expects numbers"
    (lambda () (check-type (add (tt) (num 50)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "mul expects numbers"
    (lambda () (check-type (mul (num 10) (ff)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "lt expects numbers"
    (lambda () (check-type (lt (num 25) (ff)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "if cond must be bool"
    (lambda () (check-type (ite (num 100) (tt) (ff)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "if branches mismatch"
    (lambda () (check-type (ite (tt) (num 500) (ff)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "app non-function"
    (lambda () (check-type (app (num 100) (num 300)) empty-tenv)))
  (test-error "app arg type mismatch"
    (lambda () (check-type (app (fun 'x (numT) (mul (id 'x) (num 2))) (ff)) empty-tenv)))

  (displayln "=== P1.E interp ===")
  (test "numV" (numV 42) (interp (num 42) empty-env))
  (test "boolV #t" (boolV #t) (interp (tt) empty-env))
  (test "boolV #f" (boolV #f) (interp (ff) empty-env))
  (test "add" (numV 3) (interp (add (num 1) (num 2)) empty-env))
  (test "mul" (numV 12) (interp (mul (num 3) (num 4)) empty-env))
  (test "lt true" (boolV #t) (interp (lt (num 5) (num 6)) empty-env))
  (test "lt false" (boolV #f) (interp (lt (num 9) (num 3)) empty-env))
  (test "if true" (numV 1) (interp (ite (tt) (num 1) (num 2)) empty-env))
  (test "if false" (numV 2) (interp (ite (ff) (num 1) (num 2)) empty-env))
  (test "id lookup" (numV 10) (interp (id 'x) (xtEnv 'x (numV 10) empty-env)))
  (test "app" (numV 6)
        (interp (app (fun 'x (numT) (add (id 'x) (num 1))) (num 5)) empty-env))
  (test "nested fun" (numV 7)
        (interp (app (app (fun 'x (numT) (fun 'y (numT) (add (id 'x) (id 'y))))
                          (num 3))
                     (num 4))
                empty-env))

  (displayln "=== P1.F run ===")
  (test "run +" (numV 3) (run '(+ 1 2)))
  (test "run *" (numV 12) (run '(* 3 4)))
  (test "run < true" (boolV #t) (run '(< 5 6)))
  (test "run < false" (boolV #f) (run '(< 9 3)))
  (test "run if 1" (numV 1) (run '(if true 1 2)))
  (test "run if false" (boolV #f) (run '(if false true false)))
  (test "run fun" (numV 6) (run '((fun x Number (+ x 1)) 5)))
  (test "run fun 2" (numV 20) (run '((fun x Number (* x 2)) 10)))

  ;; run rejects ill-typed programs
  (test-error "run type error" (lambda () (run '(+ true 1))))

  ;;=============================================================================
  ;; EJERCICIO 2
  ;;=============================================================================

  (displayln "=== P2.A parser ===")
  (test "numbr" (numbr 42) (parser '42))
  (test "variable" (variable 'x) (parser 'x))
  (test "plus" (plus (numbr 1) (numbr 2)) (parser '(+ 1 2)))
  (test "nil" (nil) (parser '(nil)))
  (test "cons" (conz (numbr 1) (numbr 2)) (parser '(cons 1 2)))
  (test "list 1 2 3" (conz (numbr 1) (conz (numbr 2) (conz (numbr 3) (nil))))
        (parser '(list 1 2 3)))
  (test "list empty" (nil) (parser '(list)))
  (test "list single" (conz (numbr 42) (nil)) (parser '(list 42)))
  (test "match expr" (pmatch (numbr 2)
                              (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1))
                                    (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
        (parser '(match 2 [1 1] [x 3])))
  (test-error "match no cases" (lambda () (parser '(match (list 1 2 3)))))

  (displayln "=== P2.B parse-pattern ===")
  (test "numP" (numP 3) (parse-pattern '3))
  (test "varP" (varP 'x) (parse-pattern 'x))
  (test "nilP" (nilP) (parse-pattern '(nil)))
  (test "conzP" (conzP (numP 1) (numP 2)) (parse-pattern '(cons 1 2)))
  (test "list pattern" (conzP (numP 1) (conzP (numP 2) (conzP (numP 3) (nilP))))
        (parse-pattern '(list 1 2 3)))
  (test "list empty pattern" (nilP) (parse-pattern '(list)))

  (displayln "=== P2.C generate-substs ===")
  (test "num match" (success '()) (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 3)))
  (test "num mismatch" (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern")
        (generate-substs (numP 3) (numerical 4)))
  (test "var number" (success (list (cons 'x (numerical 3))))
        (generate-substs (varP 'x) (numerical 3)))
  (test "var nil" (success (list (cons 'x (nilV))))
        (generate-substs (varP 'x) (nilV)))
  (test "num vs nil" (failure "MatchError: expected a number")
        (generate-substs (numP 3) (nilV)))
  (test "nil vs num" (failure "MatchError: expected nil")
        (generate-substs (nilP) (numerical 45)))
  (test "conz vs num" (failure "MatchError: expected a cons constructor")
        (generate-substs (conzP (varP 'x) (nilP)) (numerical 22)))
  (test "nil match" (success '()) (generate-substs (nilP) (nilV)))
  (test "cons two vars" (success (list (cons 'x (numerical 1)) (cons 'y (numerical 2))))
        (generate-substs (conzP (varP 'x) (varP 'y))
                         (conzV (numerical 1) (numerical 2))))
  (test "cons with nil" (success (list (cons 'x (numerical 1))))
        (generate-substs (conzP (varP 'x) (nilP))
                         (conzV (numerical 1) (nilV))))
  (test "cons mismatch" (failure "MatchError: given number does not match pattern")
        (generate-substs (conzP (numP 1) (nilP))
                         (conzV (numerical 2) (nilV))))

  (displayln "=== P2.D reduce ===")
  (test "numbr" (numerical 42) (reduce (numbr 42) '()))
  (test "nilV" (nilV) (reduce (nil) '()))
  (test "plus" (numerical 3) (reduce (plus (numbr 1) (numbr 2)) '()))
  (test "cons" (conzV (numerical 1) (numerical 2))
        (reduce (conz (numbr 1) (numbr 2)) '()))
  (test "list" (conzV (numerical 1) (conzV (numerical 2) (nilV)))
        (reduce (conz (numbr 1) (conz (numbr 2) (nil))) '()))
  (test "variable" (numerical 10)
        (reduce (variable 'x) (list (cons 'x (numerical 10)))))
  (test "plus with vars" (numerical 10)
        (reduce (plus (variable 'x) (variable 'y))
                (list (cons 'x (numerical 3)) (cons 'y (numerical 7)))))

  (displayln "=== P2.E reduce con match ===")
  (test "match first case fails" (numerical 3)
        (reduce (pmatch (numbr 2)
                        (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 1))
                              (cons (varP 'x) (numbr 3))))
                '()))
  (test "match nil success" (numerical 42)
        (reduce (pmatch (nil) (list (cons (nilP) (numbr 42)))) '()))
  (test "match list pattern" (numerical 10)
        (reduce (pmatch (conz (numbr 10)
                              (conz (numbr 20)
                                    (conz (numbr 30) (nil))))
                        (list (cons (conzP (varP 'x)
                                            (conzP (varP 'y)
                                                  (conzP (varP 'z) (nilP))))
                                    (variable 'x))))
                '()))
  (test-error "match no pattern matches"
    (lambda () (reduce (pmatch (nil)
                                (list (cons (numP 1) (numbr 10))
                                      (cons (numP 2) (numbr 20))))
                        '())))

  ;; Integración parser + reduce
  (displayln "=== P2 Integración parser + reduce ===")
  (test "parse+reduce match 2" (numerical 3)
        (reduce (parser '(match 2 [1 1] [x 3])) '()))
  (test "parse+reduce match list" (numerical 1)
        (reduce (parser '(match (list 1 2 3) [(list x y z) x])) '()))
  (test "parse+reduce match cons sum" (numerical 3)
        (reduce (parser '(match (cons 1 (cons 2 (nil))) [(cons x (cons y (nil))) (+ x y)])) '()))

  ;; Report
  (displayln "========================================")
  (displayln (string-append "Resultados: " (number->string passed) " pasaron, "
                            (number->string failed) " fallaron."))
  (displayln "========================================"))

(run-tests)

| Parte | Función/Tipo | Descripción |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| A | `parse-type` | Parsea un `<s-type>` a `Type`, soportando `Number`, `Boolean` y `(-> ...)` |
| B | `Expr` (extensión) | Se agregaron `fun` y `app` al tipo `Expr` y a la BNF |
| C | `parse` | Parsea sintaxis concreta a abstracta: números, booleanos, `+`, `*`, `<`, `if`, `fun`, aplicación |
| D | `check-type` | Typechecker completo con reglas de tipos y mensajes de error |
| E | `interp` | Evaluador con closures para funciones de primera clase |
| F | `run` | Integración: `parse` → `check-type` → `interp` |

**Ejercicio 2 (Pattern Matching):**

| Parte | Función/Tipo | Descripción |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| A/E | `parser` | Parsea `<s-prog>` a `Prog` con azúcar `(list ...)` y `(match ...)` |
| A/E | `Prog` (extensión) | Se agregó `(pmatch expr cases)` para pattern matching |
| B | `parse-pattern` | Parsea patrones con azúcar `(list ...)` |
| C | `generate-substs` | Genera substituciones a partir de patrón y valor, retornando `Result` |
| D/E | `reduce` | Evalúa un `Prog` a `PValue`, incluyendo `pmatch` |
| F | Respuesta | Explica por qué `generate-substs` retorna `Result` en vez de excepciones |

### `/workspace/T3_tests.rkt` — 100 tests automatizados
